sentences.text: My mother was a great inspiration to us children.
You see, she was educated.
She had a brother who was a dentist, and we were very proud of him.
He died very young.
And she had another brother who was a rabbi.
So she kept telling us that that's why she says, I broke your father's heart by moving away from the town where you were born.
Because I want you to learn to get an education.
And it was always stressed-- I mean, constantly.
If you felt like not doing your homework or something, she was always talking about it.
And it got in.
You know, you're told that you have to do it.
So basically, I was working part time.
It was a lot of trouble-- a lot of trouble.
At one time, they arrested in our house a man from Poland.
Who arrested the man?
The Russians.
I remember when people came from Warsaw, in 1939, there came some single people and a friend of ours with her husband.
And he was with them.
And he was in a Polish uniform.
He later left his uniform, and he was a civilian.
And he didn't stay too long with us.
But then he came.
He came one evening-- looked very prosperous.
And he asked-- I don't remember exactly.
He came twice or one time.
But he was-- I think he came twice.
And he asked if he could stay over the night.
He's going to Wilno.
And he said that his family is suffering terribly under German occupation in a town in Poland called Kutno, and that he's trying to help them.
That, I think, was the hand of the first trip.
Now in 1949, the war broke out the 21st of June.
No, I'm talking about '41.
He came during the Russians, and I saw him.
I mean, I saw him.
He was probably about five years older than I was. 1941, I was-- I had my exams.
And it was June.
It was the season at the lake, and it was concerts in the park.
And here I have to sit and study.
And I remember the last few days we had a radio in the house that you could get along and then all over.
And people used to come in 12 o'clock to watch the news.
And my mother was sitting, like, glued to the radio.
Because my brother at that time was not in school anymore.
The Russians took him to an officers' school.
And he was somewhere in real Russia-- not in the occupied territory.
So she was worried about him.
And it seemed like every-- I used to hear the thundering voice of Hitler.
Like, I wasn't listening to the whole speech, but I used to hear him.
And the 20-- Saturday night was-- Saturday was my last exam.
And I was, like, born again.
But it was not a good evening.
It was like my mother was crying by the-- by the radio.
My father went to work.
And with us stayed a very big-- now, all the refugees that used to be were sent to Siberia.
They caught them in the street, in different places, and they send them to Siberia.
Some of them didn't know where the rest of the family-- we were able to give them their addresses.
Because they were writing to us to getting-- they were suffering.
Some of them died, but that's another story.
At that time, they put a Russian big shot that was in charge of the-- trains-- transportation in all this area.
And they put a Jew in our house.
So he was very nice.
He spoke Jewish.
He liked my parents, and they liked him.
And he was like a friend of the family.
So he stayed with us.
And I remember that evening, everybody went to the park.
There was a open air concert.
And I was home.
And my father came in the middle of the night and said something is going on.
There were telegraphs-- telegrams-- all the time, all during the night, all the military from the hotel-- something going on.
Well, sure enough, that day they bombarded the town, and the war started.
War started.
My parents were restless.
They tried-- they thought that-- I didn't know.
How come that it even occurred to them that the Russians will evacuate us?
Because they were evacuating their own people that came to town.
That was-- that was Sunday.
And on Monday morning-- that was-- no-- Sunday.
And yeah, I wanted to go.
I thought, oh my God.
I just made my exam, so I don't even have anything to show for it.
I only had something from 1/2 a year before.
But there were-- I went with a girlfriend.
And we had to turn and go in a-- a [INAUDIBLE] is where you hide from bombs.
What do you call it?
What do you call the place over here?
Oh.
OK, anyway.
They were bombarding, and we had to hide.
And we went back home.
And every day they were bombarding.
We-- my parents packed some clothes and suitcases.
And the place where I used to have the part time job send me a note.
I should come and help out at the telegraph.
Because this was organization that they had the post offices, the telephone, the telegraph-- everything under umbrella.
I've never worked on a telegraph.
They were giving me a crash course.
I was getting heart breaking telegrams from people, from soldiers, from families.
And when I was coming home-- and my mother was worried very much that that building-- that was the nicest building in town.
It was made of marble or granite.
And it was a five story building.
So every day was-- we were afraid to go to sleep.
But the Wednesday-- now, that's from Sunday-- Wednesday it didn't stop.
I mean all day they were bombarding the town. 5 o'clock we were-- I went through the garden-- wanted to go over-- not to the main street, but to the side street.
And there was glass, and wires, and ambulances.
And it was dark-- I don't know, like dark from-- from smoke or from something.
We went back home, and it got quiet for a while.
And my parents said that we are not going to get undressed.
We're not going to sleep-- that we'll take turns.
So just the children, they-- we were laying like, with the clothes, like for 1/2 an hour, an hour.
And everybody else from the neighborhood came in and was in the garden, because they probably thought because of the shrubbery and of the trees that they'll be safe.
Now I learned that why was this area so heavily bombarded-- because I couldn't think of anything what we had in this area.
So they said that the police would not-- was just about a block away.
And about 9 o'clock, they started to hit again.
We were in the garden.
Like, my father would put his arms over all of us.
When it finished, I saw those bombs that didn't explode-- like this size-- laying all around.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how the decision was made, but my mother said, you stay all here, and don't go out.
I'll be right back.
She goes with my father.
They came.
She let the cat out.
They locked the door.
My father was carrying two suitcases and gave me a extra coat.
It was summer here at the time.
And we'll-- they said we'll walk out somewhere out of town, so-- because we couldn't-- the countryside-- they would not.
When we started to go in the-- yeah-- so we couldn't go out through the main street.
Because everything was already destroyed.
So we went.
They had barbed wire from our garden to a neighbor's-- so on.
So already then I got my feet all cut up with that wire.
And we left.
And we started to walk.
While we were walking, and we got-- Who was with you at the time?
My mother, my father, and my two sisters.
And when we got to a area, it was called the [NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH] was like, the-- the-- the bad part of town.
We saw people with blankets half asleep, because-- running the same way the way we were running.
And it was so many people.
When it's-- when-- and we saw there was no use stopping-- that if everybody's running.
So we kept going, and we kept going.
There were, like, small villages, one after another one.
And my mother said, well, we-- in all the small towns we had relatives.
She said we will stop by.
I don't remember the towns.
I try always to remember them in order.
I can't remember.
But we ran-- we walked, like, till the first town.
We walked to the first town, and we came to a town that my mother-- I knew from my mother's stories.
I don't remember if we knew anybody there.
People were-- all the Jews were all packed to leave.
Now, we were going to the Russian border, to the previous border-- the one that existed till 1939.
This-- the rumors were that if we cross the border, we'll be safer.

I mean, Germany would only take away back the territory that-- that they gave to Russians during the pact, and that we'll get the-- once we cross the border the rumors were that nobody-- the Russians don't let nobody in.
I mean, rumors were plenty.
We came to the first town, and we stayed in a place that I've never been, I'm sure.
But all day, I had this eerie feeling.
I knew exactly where everything is there.
It was a barn.
I knew everything where it is.
I couldn't-- I-- I just thought that if I would believe in a previous life, that I've been there.
I'll never forget it.
I knew where the rooms were.
I knew where the place where to go to the toilet.
It wasn't really-- and I knew.
And I haven't been in my whole life in a barn like that-- not only somebody else's and everything.
Anyway, we stayed only a day.
And we came to another small town.
And we-- my mother had a cousin.
He was a dentist there.
He had a wife and one son.
Rothenburg.
Of the-- of the-- of the town?
I know I-- I-- I-- there is a few towns I knew we had.
I don't remember.
And this person survived.
So Tyczyn was one.
We went in Tyczyn.
So we were-- or in-- Rakitna.
Rakitna was where the barn was.
And I don't remember.
It's one of the small towns right in that area from-- I even got a map.
And I many times sit to trigger my-- if I can trigger my memory, where exactly-- not because of that-- of another problem I wanted to know.
Anyway, we stayed there.
They were all packed to run away.
We stayed overnight, and we were coming.
They said there is no use coming close to the border.
They wouldn't let us in.
But the Germans were already-- we were getting news that the Germans already occupied the town where we are, and they are going real fast.
By that time from then, the wire that I stepped on-- I had a blister like this on my foot.
And the whole leg was getting red.
And I couldn't walk.
Now we were going to the border.
The Russians were going to the front-- the same road we took on the edge of a forest.
We were walking, and they were-- and they were with the tanks and with them.
And we tried to stop some of those trucks that were going in the direction.
They said they-- they can be shot if they take some civilians.
And I kept crying.
And I said, why don't you go?
It was a real panic.
People-- old people were saying goodbye to their children, to sons.
And I said, why don't you keep going, and I'll see if I can get a ride?
And my mother said, we're going all or we're staying all.
We're not going to leave you.
There was a-- I worked with a Russian in the-- he was an accountant in the bookkeeping department.
He was a nice man when he was sober.
He was mostly drunk.
And I see him in uniform on one of those heavy trucks.
And it's fully loaded.
And he stopped.
And then he stopped.
And I said-- he says what happened?
I-- I tell him that I'm here with my whole family.
I said, can you take me to the-- he says, I can't take you.
I could take you by yourself, but I can't take the whole family.
And they all decided no, that I'm not going by myself.
So he said, you wait with me.
I'll deliver this, and I'll come for you.
And he did.
He came with a truck.
He put us all in.
And we came in on the other side of the border at a town called Zhytomyr, I think-- no, Zhytomyr, no-- Nova-- Novohrad-Volynskyi-- Novohrad-Volynskyi.
Yeah, that's already the Russia.
And the year was 1941.
Now, it only was a short time from Wednesday when we left.
I don't know if it was a week, or what-- eight days-- I don't know.
But in between, before we cross the Russian border at night, we got lost in a-- in a wooded area.
And we didn't know where we were-- started to go.
And people say we're going back.
They see some lights.
We're going back.
We're going back to the Germans.
So they saw a group of Russian military, sitting, like, in a group.
And they said, we have to ask them.
We have to ask them where to go.
They tell me I should go and ask.
I went over.
And I told them we wanna go down in there where the Russian border.
And he showed us a direction.
That's why when we started to go, the people said, we're going back.
And I-- and I right away, I thought, those guys didn't look good.
Their uniforms were like this.
They spoke Russian, like somebody who didn't know Russian very well-- with an accent.
Well, anyway, we turned around.
We were going in a bad direction.
They were descendents-- Germans-- that they-- they really had to tell their military.
I mean, we were not important.
But once we ask, they send us back.
Anyway, when we came to Novohrad-Volynskyi, the Jews were-- it was a very Jewish town.
Here, on the way, we had to eat.
Nobody wanted to take from the peasants Russian money.
And they said, when Hitler will come, he'll pay you for everything.
That's the-- nobody.
We-- we gave away a watch for a piece of bread.
We gave away everything.
Nobody wanted to sell anything.
But when we were in Novohrad-Volynskyi, we stayed with a Jewish family.
They-- somebody opened up that infectious thing I had, and bandaged it, and-- and whatever.
And my pain-- because at the end, they were carrying me.
I couldn't walk at all.
And then we were about to take a train to take us deeper in Russia.
Here, on the way, the suitcases were dropped a long time ago.
They were dropped the first few hours when we were walking, because it was too heavy to carry.
And at one time in the morning, the first two days, the Germans-- we were walking like, on the road, tired already-- exhausted.
The Germans came down like real low with a-- with a airplane-- never saw them so low.
And we all this-- everybody dispersed.
They were screaming high-- fall to the ground.
Fall to the ground.
I was with my-- one of my sisters.
My parents weren't with me.
My youngest sister wasn't with me.
In a ditch, we fell in.
And they went down with machine guns.
And we got up.
There were so many dead people.
And I didn't know where my mother and father-- and where my sister.
Well, anyway, we found each other.
And that's-- that was still in the beginning.
And when we came to Novohrad-Volynskyi-- and I don't remember.
We stayed there a few days, because I really couldn't walk at all.
And we went on a train.
And we stayed in the train for days.
And one time they bombarded the railroad station, but not exactly our train.
But you had to run and hide.
Eventually, we left.
And we came to a town called Kiev, which is today the capital of Ukraine.
It's a beautiful city.
You didn't-- you didn't feel that there is a war.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --we had to go with a little-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --with the-- this wasn't a ship, really.
They were transporting strawberries with it, probably from one town to another one.
They said they'll take us this, and take us somewhere else.
I-- I mean, I lost track.
I-- I-- maybe I didn't keep track all together, you know?
When you're with parents, you just let them think about it.
So we did it-- we'll-- that they'll take us somewhere else.
And when we left there was no place to stay, anyway.
We came to a town called Kuybyshev.
Kuybyshev was a known place, a Jewish city.
It was a beautiful summer night.
We were so exhausted.
There was a rocky area, somewhere-- like some grass, but there were rocks in it.
And I remember laying down there.
And we were all laying down.
I think it was somewhere close to a train.
And people were-- it was summer night.
Girls were going out with boys and laughing on dates.
And there was music playing.
And it was like there was no war.
And here, we're already exhausted, going from one place to another one.
And my mother, as I told you in the beginning, she was a very fragile woman.
She maybe weighed at 100 pounds.
And she didn't complain-- nothing.
She was the first to go, the first to direct, the first to-- to help out.
And I remember feeling very sad.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we came to a town.
From Kuybyshev, we came Zhytomyr.
I hope I did not change the names.
This was a-- a gentile town-- a lot of Ukrainian.
Right away, I could see that they went.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Meanwhile, I don't remember exactly where.
Or-- we met a family that we were very close with them, but I don't remember exactly where.
We were not with them there.
But I think after Zhytomyr, we met them.
That was a mother and a father.
There was one daughter that was my age.
And we knew each other from that small town.
And another daughter who was pregnant, her husband was-- that's another story what-- where her husband was.
And we were clutching to each other, that we'll stay together.
And we came to a big city, Kharkiv.
And at Kharkiv, they told us they're going to send us to the middle Asia, like Samarkand or Tashkent-- some-- because it's warm.
And the Russian winter is very severe.
And we were running the way we were.
That's what we had on us.
Meanwhile, the woman started to deliver a baby.
And-- and-- no.
We met them-- oh, in Kharkiv.
No.
I have to tell you about Kharkiv.
Before we came to Kharkiv, they mobilized us to take down the crop from the fields, because the Germans are closing in and they don't want to leave any food for the Germans.
And we have to feed the army.
And that it's your patriotic duty to-- to work on the fields.
So going there, we stop in a town, a small town.
And we see a bunch of students-- girls-- that they took from the Kharkiv University to take down the-- to do the same job.
And we stayed the day there, and we got very close with the girls.
And when we said goodbye, we didn't know where they're taking us.
They said, remember, this is the address.
You see where we live.
If you get-- they didn't know what we're going to do or nothing.
When you can-- I don't remember exactly-- but we promised that we'll see them again.
They took us to a place-- a-- a barn.
A lot of people were there with a lot of-- with straw.
I don't remember if the animals were there or the animals were removed.
And they told us, gave us that patriotic speech that we have to help.
And I was very patriotic-- I mean, that we have to remove from the fields everything.
My middle sister, that one that's a nurse, she had a habit that she needed a nap in the afternoon.
And they took us.
First, we've never been to a field.
I didn't know what are potatoes and what are weeds.
I didn't know what are carrots and what are weeds.
So we all worked on this, and then she fell asleep on the-- in the field.
She fell-- she sat down and very comfortably fell asleep.
She was always a rebel.
I mean, she did what she wanted.
And they said they can-- can shot us for this.
So she said, anyway, I don't know what a potato is and what is the carrot.
I was probably pulling out the wrong things anyway.
So it's not that I-- that I really robbed them of a worker.
They put me on a tractor.
I'll never forget it.
Did you ever see a-- probably, you-- the tractors today are different like then.
That straw was cutting in in my skin, and it was going fast.
It's like-- and I was working 10 hours.
And you have to keep putting and feeding it.
And this straw was getting in all over for 10 hours.
After 10 hours, they got us all together, taking to the-- I couldn't walk on my feet.
I walked-- I worked like that a few days.
And then it started to rain.
They took away our passports.
Well, I didn't had a passport yet.
But my parents had a passport, which was the most precious thing you can have in Russia, because if you've got the wrong passport, I mean, you-- you're going to Siberia.
So by the time my parents got a passport, it was one of the biggest things at that time.
So they took away the passports, and we had no documents.
And it started to rain.
And we only had this barn.
When we had to eat, you had to work-- to walk to a field.
And there was like a dining.
They used to cook a soup with some kind of a heavy-- I don't know-- grits, or something.
And that was the meal.
That was the meal what we ate.
I remember there was the one Russian.
He wasn't working.
So my father says, how come you're not working?
He says, wherever I come, they ask me my profession.
[NON-ENGLISH] I tell them I'm-- that my profession is sick.
[NON-ENGLISH] So they don't take me to work, he says.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyways, it started to rain real bad.
We couldn't even walk to the dining room.
My father had a pair of officers boots, and he was wear it.
My father was a tall man.
So when I put the boots, they were probably out till here.
But we were taking turns, because you put in your foot in the mud, and you couldn't drag it out.
And my mother says, if we stay where-- we stay here, we'll finished.
We never get out of here.
And my father went to the office and tried to get our documents-- nothing doing.
I don't know how, but they got our passports, woke us up at 12 o'clock at night.
Put all your clothes on, and we'll leave.
And only us, my mother, father, and the two sisters, and myself, we started to walk.
It was raining.
After a while, we lost-- we didn't know exactly where we're going.
Our idea was to go back to that town where we were.
So, again, they covered us up from the rain with-- with themselves.
We were sitting under the trees till it started to get-- it was pitch dark.
Because they have-- they had blackouts.
I mean, even if you had lights, you couldn't use them.
I don't remember, but I remember it was black.
So it started to get a little bit light.
We started to walk.
And we came to this town.
We knocked on the window from the girls.
They pulled us all in through the windows.
And they gave us dry clothes.
They put our clothes to dry, because we were soaking wet.
And we were trying to get on a train.
And we were told that the train there-- it's a small town.
It does not stop, but it slows down.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I don't remember how long it took.
It took more than a day.
Like, the kids went on-- went on before.
And then my mother wouldn't go before my father, so my father was on the train.
And by the time my mother-- they were holding her by two hand.
It wasn't a passenger train.
It was a cattle train.
Were holding her by the two hands.
And the train was going so fast.
And they dragged her in inside.
After it, we came to Kharkiv.
And that's where we met that other family-- the friends.
And she started-- and we decided that we-- whatever happens, we're going together.
And she-- or on the train we found them.
I don't know.
I know when we were in Kharkiv, we already were with them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She-- she started to have labor pains and was taking to the hospital.
And we-- and the idea was that we're going to Middle Asia.
And she had a little boy.
And they gave her a-- a piece of blanket to wrap him.
And it started to get cold.
It was August-- end of August.
And it started to get very cold.
And--
